element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Spatial planning to align boxes with targets
Navigation constraints and box blocking against walls
Multi-move planning to avoid permanent traps; undo mechanic for recovery
Complex sequencing across multiple interdependent boxes

typical_relief_triggers: Successful box placement on storage location
Completing puzzle and advancing to next level
typical_tension_triggers: Pushing a box into corner or against wall
Running out of viable move sequences
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Contemplative, self-paced; tension builds through planning depth rather than time pressure

difficulty_curve.early_game: Simple 1-2 box puzzles teaching push mechanic and directional constraint
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Deadlock avoidance becomes primary challenge; multi-box sequencing introduced
difficulty_curve.late_game: Complex spatial topology with high consequence for incorrect moves; multiple interdependent boxes
notable_spikes: Introduction of deadlock concept and consequence permanence
Transition from single-box to multi-box puzzles
Levels designed with dead ends as environmental features rather than careless player mistakes

notable_level_patterns.description: Early levels (first 5-20) introduce mechanics through spatial simplicity: single or two boxes, wide open warehouse areas, minimal wall complexity. Forces mastery of push-only mechanic before deadlock risk.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Level 1 of original Sokoban (NEC PC-8801 version)

notable_level_patterns.description: Transition levels deliberately create corner-trap and wall-pin scenarios. Player experiences deadlock, learns undo mechanic, then replays with foresight.
Mid-to-late game levels use corridor and chamber design to force specific box-pushing order. Player must recognize dependency chains across multiple boxes.
Late-game levels pack multiple boxes, storage locations, and walls such that almost any move has irreversible consequences. Requires extensive lookahead planning.
